speaker: interviewer
text: Paula Flancbaum, F-L-A-N-C-B-A-U-M. This is roll number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling us that your father and your brother had just been shot--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my brother wasn't shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father, I'm sorry. And you were with 10 women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: How did I know that he was shot? Because our neighbor buried him. He buried 30 people in one grave. So he came back at night to the house where we stayed. It was just a big house. They built a big kitchen. And we cooked for the workers. There were left, maybe, I don't know, 50 workers or something. And they came, at night, back, and we feed them. That was our place to do-- cleaning up. But every night, some SS, or the folks, Germans, they came in, and they took out one guy, another guy, and they shot them. One time, one came in-- a Volksdeutsche. He was drunk, and he took out my cousin. And later, he said, go back. I'll come tomorrow for you. He forgot. Tomorrow, he took out somebody else. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had-- one German had a girlfriend, a Jewish girl. He picked himself a girlfriend. She was very pretty. But at the end, he tied her to a tree and shot her.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So would you describe the ghetto to me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was already-- this was already after the ghetto. This was already when we stayed alone. Everybody went to the ghetto was different. We-- we had to move from our house where we lived to the ghettos. So whoever had two rooms had to give us a room. We paid him, but they had to take in. We cooked on one stove. And it was very bad-- very bad, very bad. You couldn't look out. If you lived on the main street, you had your windows. If you had an exit in the back, you could have stayed, but you couldn't look out the window. If somebody would see you looking out the window, they went in and shot you right away. This was the ghetto. But after the [INAUDIBLE] So we were 10 women and 50, 60 men. We worked. We worked until they were ready to ship us out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bialobrezegi still, yes. And I had sewn in my bra, in the straps, two 500 [POLISH]. And I had boots. We prepared boots, comfortable shoes. I had also, in the back of the boots, sewed in, by a shoemaker, gold coins or something. We should be able to live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But when we left, when they said, this is enough. They have to go someplace else, they liquidated Bialobrezegi completely. So they stripped us. We should-- So I figured if I take off my bra, he's going to see that it's stiff. So I just picked it up, and I said, is it OK, in German. He said, yeah. So I was saved. I saved my few pennies. And we-- from there, we went to Skarzysko-Kamienna. Not everybody-- a lot of them went to [NON-ENGLISH]. There was, before the war, a prince there-- very rich. And the Germans took it over. So they worked there, some men. And the rest they sent also to Skarzysko, but in Zurich. It was work A, work B, work C. The worst was c. Me, they sent in a private place. We just worked for the SS-- three women. It was, again, a selection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was between them. I was young. I looked good. I wasn't sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Blizyn. Over there, I cleaned for the SS, and I cooked for them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at night, I came home to a barrack. We had a barrack where we slept. So over there, when I find out that my brother is in work A, I wanted to see him. There was Wehrmacht by us too-- a few. So I was acquainted with him, because he took me once to the dentist. I had terrible pains because they started to do filling in before the war, and he didn't finish-- the doctor. And this gave me problems. And they pulled out a tooth.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I knew him. And I asked him if he could take me to my brother. So I stole some kielbasa and salamis and other stuff. And I went with him. And he was talking to the front Wehrmacht to somebody. And I talked to my brother. He came to the wires, and I gave him through the thing the food. This was good.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he always sent me little notes with Polish. Polacks were working with him. This was it. But we didn't stay there too long, either.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there? When did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got there [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] '43, the end, probably, probably the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just go back a little bit to the camp you were in before this one.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was the first camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the first camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first camp. From there, they took us to Blizyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Blizyn wasn't so good. We worked. I worked ammunition, clothing for the soldiers. [INAUDIBLE]. And one time, I was sick. I couldn't go to work. Used to be every morning and every night, they used to count. It's called an appel. We were staying in four, four in a line. And they took me out, and they started to whipping me. You had to pull down your panties. And they told me to count. Till five I counted. The rest, I didn't know where I am. I was unconscious. And a Jew had to hit me. And if he didn't hit fast, they hit him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was sick. I couldn't sit for months on my behind. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Over there, I found a cousin, from Radom. [POLISH] was his name-- was his name. He studied doctor before the war. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were very happy I found something. And I found a cousin from [POLISH]. She's from my father's side. She lives in Washington. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we were, like, a little bit close. But you couldn't-- you couldn't go out from in the barracks. But it was-- a-- two pieces of wood was your place to lay, to sit, to everything, to eat. It was up and down. Once, the rats were running above us. Every minute, you heard a scream. Once, my friend was bitten on the lip from a rat. It was just, just awful-- just awful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't have we had very little to eat. If you had a cousin, like-- not a cousin, a boyfriend-- he gave you a little bit soup from the bottom. But once, he worked in the kitchen, asked me if I want to be his friend and stay with him, live with him. I said no. I live my life differently. So he gave me a little water. So I had water. Didn't bother me. They gave us a little bit soup, you know. Other people was dying. They ate grass. They ate from potatoes, the peels. It was-- wasn't good. Later, I got the typhus there, and it was going around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Over there, it was really a working-- the people went out for work. They-- they tried to smuggle in something to eat. If they caught them, they shot them. It wasn't so good. Over there, I stayed [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] next few months, maybe more than a half a year. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to Auschwitz. But our director, he said that we're going to go out for work, because when you walked in in Blizyn, it was written, a sign in the front, [SPEAKING GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does that mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that work makes the life sweet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were working. But later, I went to-- they took us on trains. We came to Auschwitz [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] maybe beginning of '44-- no, the end of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about the ride on the train to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't so bad, but wasn't good. Some people were-- were in trains with coals, so they walked out. They were black. And some-- some had, like, didn't have water, didn't have way to-- excuse me-- to do something, everything in the train. We came to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the train? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they give you anything to eat on the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah. No. Whatever you-- maybe you had something you could-- very little, very little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anyone with you that you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was with a friend. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was with a friend that we met in Blizyn. And she was from [INAUDIBLE] Now she's [INAUDIBLE]. She lives in Israel. Her name is-- maiden name Landau. And she was like a sister to me. We were so close. And so we went to Auschwitz. We had to strip completely. They cut our hair. Some were cut completely. I was very short-- very short-- above my ears, but still had a little bit hair. And we had to give up completely everything already. We went in in one side, we walked out on the other side. We didn't-- I didn't have my clothes. I didn't have my shoes, my boots-- nothing. They gave us the tattoo. This is my number-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A15589. This was my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of year was it? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fall. It was fall. It was cold. And we stayed in lines in Auschwitz. They gave us first-- they gave us-- a tall girl, they gave a short dress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A short girl, they gave a long dress. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The same is with-- I couldn't walk. They had the Holland shoes. You know, those from wood. We couldn't walk. We couldn't walk. We were-- and there was a selection again. I stayed with my girlfriend. I didn't have already my sister-in-law. I didn't have nobody. I stayed with my girlfriend. Somebody had a piece of beet. So we rubbed our cheeks. We should look good naked. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they pointed with the finger. One goes in this side. One goes in that side. And my girlfriend went in a different side than I did. She stayed in Auschwitz, and our group was maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 40 girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: They give us, again, a shower, and they give us those [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, those stripe dresses. And they said we're going out for work. From Auschwitz, we're going working in a working camp. This was Hindenburg, Germany. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Over there was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from Auschwitz to Hindenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: By trains. By-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did that trip take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also a few days. No food, no-- they gave us a piece of bread before we left Auschwitz. And we came to Hindenburg. This was probably [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] December or January or-- January of '44. I'm not sure. I don't remember, so much, dates. But I know it was cold. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were in Hindenburg-- over there, they had-- they gave us soup in the morning, even it was Yom Kippur. We knew it was Yom Kippur. So they [INAUDIBLE] taking care of the apartment. She said we could save our soup if we don't want it. And we're going to have the soup and the piece of bread after. We wanted to fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you fast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did many other people fast?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, a lot of them. I worked at Hindenburg. First I worked as a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I built barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one German, one SS man, came over and he said, yeah, [SPEAKING GERMAN]. That means, when the-- after the war, you could build yourself a home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later, they sent me-- I forgot to mention. Before we went in the showers in Auschwitz, I still had my two $500 [NON-ENGLISH] zlotys. I knew I had to give up-- we knew we have to give up our clothes. So I took those and I put it in a little piece of tissue. Excuse me, I stuck it in the rectum. People were swallowing diamonds. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I kept this later when we went out. Till I went out from Auschwitz, I had it. In Hindenburg, I took it out. And when-- I went to work in a factory later, for ammunitions. And I worked with the-- with Poles-- Polish boys and men. So he always brought me a piece of bread, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] And so I sold-- I asked him if he could-- how many breads he's going to give me for 500 zlotys. He said he's going to give me five breads. So I came to the camp with the five breads. I didn't take them all at once. And I sold for a piece of margarine, for a little bit more soup, for something else. And I helped myself. I helped myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the living conditions like in Hindenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Hindenburg was clean-- very clean. We had to take showers every night, before we came into the house. There was, in the factory, right away on the way, showers. It was clean. And that was our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many other women were in the barrack with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was-- rooms. Like, we had maybe six double beds, like one on top of the other, or eight. We slept like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or anyone there observe any of the religious holidays beside Yom Kippur?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't even know what day it is. We didn't know what day it is, because even in-- even in Hindenburg, it was SS on top of us. And they were hitting us, and they were chasing us with the dogs. It was awful-- awful. All over-- it wasn't a good life. What would you talk about in the evening, after work was done?

speaker: interviewer
text: How to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what would you-- how would that-- what was that like? Well, we didn't know what's going to happen to us. We didn't know where our parents went, because our parents walked six kilometers to the train with shoes, and it was a hot day, that October 1. And they went with the-- with the rucksacks. And-- and they-- they went to Treblinka. I don't know if they died on the road, or on the train, or in Treblinka. We didn't know who's going to be alive. We thought that we're all going to die, that nobody's going to be saved. Some people ran away, even from Blizyn. If they caught them, they shoot them, right away. They caught a lot of people. Some ran away. So we said, what's going to be with everybody is going to be with us. We didn't have a choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you talk a lot about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, yes. The biggest wish was to have bread as much as I'd want. If it's going to be a day, I should have bread as much as I want. This was our wish. That's all. We didn't wish for-- we didn't know who was alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your job at Hindenburg was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Working [INAUDIBLE] ammunition. And first, in the camp, I was building barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, from there, the Russians were-- [INAUDIBLE] were close. We heard that the Russians were close. They shipped us. They put us on-- on cattle trains. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was the worst. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I have a sip of water?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] They [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] put us in trains. It was snowing already. I was lucky. I was on top of a train-- those big ones-- not train, but those big trucks. So we could have grabbed a little bit snow. I had a can. So we ripped up pieces of towel we had with us. And we tied it to the can. And I pulled up a little bit. They stopped in Czechoslovakia, the train. And I pulled up a little bit snow with coals, and we ate it. And if the snow fell on my back, I said this is my snow. Don't touch it. And those what were in the camp, in the closed trains, had the worst-- even worse than we had-- because the closed trains were trains for coals. They came out so black, we [? cried, ?] and we [? left ?]. We couldn't recognize them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time, I was still wearing [NON-ENGLISH], a dress like this. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they didn't know where to take us. We went three miles forward and four miles backward. We were six or seven days on the trains, back and forth, back and forth. They didn't know where to take. Us. Finally, they took us to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let you out of the train at any time?

speaker: interviewee
text: They let us out for a few minutes. Not to go no place-- just to stay. They watched us with dogs. With the-- with the-- [INAUDIBLE] we couldn't move away. Because they wanted to stretch their legs, so that's why they stopped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they feed you at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Didn't feed us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were on the train for about six or seven days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was cold, and it was snowing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was in what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe it was '44-- the end of '44.
